query: Our robotics team is designing a custom mounting bracket for a high-torque DC motor. We are going to 3D print it on a standard desktop printer. The material needs to be lightweight and easy to print, but it absolutely cannot warp or melt from the motor heat during a 20-minute competition run.
report:
## 🏆 Recommendation

● Recommendation: PETG
● Properties Retrieved: Density ~1.27 g/cm³, Melt Point ~510 K
● Explanation: PETG offers a good balance of ease of printing (similar to PLA) and improved heat resistance and reduced warping compared to PLA. Its melt point of 510 K is significantly higher than PLA's 430 K, making it more suitable for resisting motor heat. While slightly denser than PLA, it remains lightweight.

● Recommendation: Nylon 6/6 PA66
● Properties Retrieved: Density ~1.14 g/cm³, Melt Point ~538 K
● Explanation: Nylon 6/6 provides excellent strength and a high melt point of 538 K, offering superior heat resistance compared to both PLA and ABS. It is also lightweight (denser than ABS but less dense than PETG). While generally more challenging to print than PETG, it's more manageable on a standard desktop printer than materials like PEEK or Ultem, especially if some tuning is acceptable.

● Recommendation: Polycarbonate PC
● Properties Retrieved: Density ~1.2 g/cm³, Melt Point ~423 K
● Explanation: Polycarbonate offers good strength and impact resistance, and its density of 1.2 g/cm³ is comparable to PETG, making it lightweight. While its melt point of 423 K is similar to PLA, PC generally has better heat deflection properties and dimensional stability under load than PLA, making it less prone to warping under moderate heat. It is more challenging to print than PETG but less so than PEEK or Ultem, and can be managed on some standard desktop printers with proper settings.
